elements: triangle representing the 2D simplex (equilateral triangle on the xy-plane)
vertices A, B, and C of the simplex
barycentric coordinates represented as a point P within the triangle
coordinate axes: x-axis and y-axis
annotations and numerical labels for each vertex
lines connecting P to each vertex demonstrating how barycentric coordinates are determined

interactions.teaches: Dragging point P within the triangle demonstrates how its position relates to the barycentric coordinates. The coordinates change to reflect P’s proportionate distance to the vertices.
Hovering over any vertex will briefly display its numerical annotation, reinforcing the concept that each vertex has a fixed barycentric coordinate of either 1 or 0 for its respective component.

demo_steps.title: Understanding Barycentric Coordinates
demo_steps.narration: We begin with point P inside the triangle. Its position is expressed entirely in terms of distances to vertices A, B, and C, showing that anywhere P is placed, the sum of its barycentric coordinates equals 1.

demo_steps.title: Move P to Vertex
demo_steps.narration: Here, as you drag point P directly over vertex A, its barycentric coordinates become (1, 0, 0). This illustrates that P is exactly at A and none of the distance is apportioned to B or C.

demo_steps.title: Midpoint Balancing
demo_steps.narration: Now drag P to the midpoint of edge BC. Notice that the barycentric coordinates change to (0, 0.5, 0.5), showing that P is equally influenced by B and C, with no influence from A.

plan.camera_suggestion: Top-down orthographic view centered on the simplex triangle, closely cropped to fit the triangle within the view without extra whitespace, but maintaining label visibility

inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips for vertex annotations only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: ensure the orthographic projection reflects the front view where all labels, vertex points, and the triangle are clearly visible

plan.notes: Use an orthographic camera to maintain correct proportions of the triangle and relations within; ensure vertex labels are properly aligned and consistent with annotations in the source figure.

plan.contextChunk:
classes, is present in the image. This is typical in settings where most
of the images contain a single large object. Multiclass classification
with exclusive classes results is given in the following constraint:

$$\sum_{c=1}^K \hat{y}_c=1 
$${#eq-object_recognition-sum_to_one_constraint}

where $0>\hat{y}_c>1$. In the toy example with three classes shown
previously, the valid solutions for
$\hat{\mathbf{y}}=[\hat{y}_1, \hat{y}_2, \hat{y}_3]^\mathsf{T}$ are
constrained to lie within a **simplex**.

:::{.column-margin}
Simplex with three exclusive classes:

![](figures/object_recognition/simplex_v2.png){width="100%"}
:::


Under this formulation, the function $f$ is a mapping
$f:\mathbb{R}^{N \times M \times 3} \rightarrow \vartriangle^{K-1}$ from
the set of red-green-blue (RGB) images to the $(K-1)$-dimensional
simplex, $\vartriangle^{K-1}$.

The function $f$ is constrained to belong to a family of possible
functions. For instance, $f$ might belong to the space of all the
functions that can be built with a neural network. In such a case the
function is specified by the network parameters $\theta: f_\theta$. When
using a neural net, the vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is usually computed as
the output of a softmax layer.

elements: 3D coordinate system with axes y1, y2, y3
red shaded triangular simplex
label Bike along y2-axis
label Car along y1-axis
label Bird along y3-axis
0 to 1 tick marks on each axis

interactions.teaches: Dragging the point on the simplex shows how the components of vector y change while maintaining their sum to 1, illustrating the constraint of multiclass classification with exclusive classes.

demo_steps.title: Basic setup
demo_steps.narration: This 3D simplex shows a probability distribution across three exclusive classes: Bike, Car, and Bird. Any point within the triangle must sum its coordinates to 1, meaning it represents a valid probability vector in a multiclass classification setting.

demo_steps.title: Moving toward a class
demo_steps.narration: Dragging the point toward the Bike vertex increases the probability for the Bike class while reducing the probabilities for Car and Bird. This visualization helps in understanding how classification confidence changes.

demo_steps.title: Equal probability point
demo_steps.narration: Position the point at the center of the simplex. Here, the distribution is uniform — each class (Bike, Car, Bird) has an equal probability of 1/3, representing maximum uncertainty in classification.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing view aligned with the simplex plane, slightly elevated, showing the entire triangle and labels clearly.

inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

interactionPlan.notes: Ensure the simplex remains an accurate representation visually, allowing the drag interaction to constrain the point within the triangle. Use perspective camera settings to align with the apparent original viewpoint.

elements: 3D coordinate system with axes labeled y1, y2, y3
red shaded triangular simplex
label Bike along y1-axis
label Car along y2-axis
label Bird along y3-axis
tick marks from 0 to 1 on each axis

interactions.teaches: Dragging the point within the simplex demonstrates how the probability distribution across the three classes changes while maintaining a total sum of 1, illustrating the concept of multiclass classification with exclusive class membership.

demo_steps.title: Introducing the simplex
demo_steps.narration: This is a 3D representation of a probability simplex for three classes: Bike, Car, and Bird. Any point within this triangle represents a valid probability distribution where the total is always 1.

demo_steps.title: Approaching a class vertex
demo_steps.narration: As you drag the point closer to the Bike vertex, the probability for the Bike class increases, indicating higher confidence in classifying an image as a Bike, while the confidence for Car and Bird decreases.

demo_steps.title: Center equidistance
demo_steps.narration: Dragging to the center of the triangle results in each class (Bike, Car, Bird) having a probability of 1/3, representing maximum uncertainty or equal likelihood among the three classes.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front-facing orthographic view, slightly elevated, capturing the entire simplex and ensuring all labels are legible.

plan.notes: Use an orthographic camera to maintain the fidelity of proportions. Ensure the simplex maintains its red shading, and constrain the drag interaction so the point cannot leave the triangular area.
